questions.text: What type of sentence is this?When I go swimming, I have to keep my eyes closed underwater.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This sentence is an example of complex sentence.It has an independent clause (main clause): "I have to keep my eyes closed underwater" and a dependent clause (subordinate clause): "When I go swimming".The dependent clause "When I go swimming" cannot stand alone as a complete sentence, and it relies on the main clause to make sense. The word "when" is a subordinating conjunction that introduces the dependent clause and indicates the condition or circumstance under which the main action takes place.
Simple sentence has only one independent clause, without any dependent clauses.
Compound sentence has two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction.
This is incorrect because the sentence is a complex type.

questions.explanation: This sentence is an example of complex sentence.It has an independent clause (main clause): "I have to keep my eyes closed underwater" and a dependent clause (subordinate clause): "When I go swimming".The dependent clause "When I go swimming" cannot stand alone as a complete sentence, and it relies on the main clause to make sense. The word "when" is a subordinating conjunction that introduces the dependent clause and indicates the condition or circumstance under which the main action takes place.

questions.text: What type of sentence is this?Our car broke down, so we came last.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This not a complex sentence. A complex sentence has one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
This not a simple sentence. A simple sentence has only one independent clause.

This sentence is an example of a compound sentence.It contains two independent clauses (main clauses):"Our car broke down" and "we came last"These clauses are joined by the coordinating conjunction "so", which indicates a cause-and-effect relationship between the two clauses. Both clauses could stand alone as separate sentences, but they are combined to show the relationship between the car breaking down and the consequence of arriving late.
This not a compound-complex sentence. A compound-complex sentence has two or more independent clauses and at least one dependent clause.

questions.explanation: This sentence is an example of a compound sentence.It contains two independent clauses (main clauses):"Our car broke down" and "we came last"These clauses are joined by the coordinating conjunction "so", which indicates a cause-and-effect relationship between the two clauses. Both clauses could stand alone as separate sentences, but they are combined to show the relationship between the car breaking down and the consequence of arriving late.

options.text: E-mail is a relatively new mean of communication.
options.explanation: The word "mean" should be plural, which should be "means."

options.text: E-mail is a relatively new mean to communication.
options.explanation: "Means" should be used instead of "mean." Additionally, the preposition "to" should be "of" to correctly express the idea of communication.

options.text: E-mail is a relatively new means of communication.
options.explanation: The correct form is "means of communication" because it refers to the method or way by which communication is carried out, in this case, through e-mail. The term "means" is plural to indicate that there are multiple ways or methods available for communication.It's important to note that the correct option also includes the word "relatively" to indicate that e-mail is a comparatively new method of communication, implying that it has not been in existence for an extensive period of time.

options.text: E-mail is relatively new means to communication.
options.explanation: The preposition "to" should be "of" to correctly express the idea of communication. Article "a" should be used before relatively to express the correct idea of communication.

questions.explanation: Options A and B are wrong because they use “mean” which is not an appropriate word here it should be “means” because “means” implies “a system to convey or a way to do something” while “mean” implies “signify/denote or to aim/intend” something.Option D is wrong because it does not use the article “a” before relatively, which makes the sentence incomplete. Secondly, there is always “means of” something like “way of doing something”, never “means to” do something.

questions.text: ‘She always carried an umbrella’.The sentence indicates _ tense.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence "She always carried an umbrella" indicates the past tense.Specifically, it is in the simple past tense. This tense is used to describe actions or events that were completed at a definite time in the past. The verb "carried" is the past tense form of "carry." The word "always" suggests a habitual action in the past.So, the sentence expresses a recurring action that took place in the past.

The sentence "She always carried an umbrella" indicates the past tense.Specifically, it is in the simple past tense. This tense is used to describe actions or events that were completed at a definite time in the past. The verb "carried" is the past tense form of "carry." The word "always" suggests a habitual action in the past.So, the sentence expresses a recurring action that took place in the past.

This tense is used to describe actions that occurred before another action in the past. The sentence would need to be in the past perfect form, e.g., "She had carried an umbrella before she got to the party" or "She had always carried an umbrella until she lost it".

This tense is used to describe actions that started in the past and continue up to the present moment. The sentence would need to be in the present perfect form, e.g., "She has carried an umbrella every day this week" or "She has always carried an umbrella since she moved to London".

questions.explanation: The sentence "She always carried an umbrella" indicates the past tense.Specifically, it is in the simple past tense. This tense is used to describe actions or events that were completed at a definite time in the past. The verb "carried" is the past tense form of "carry." The word "always" suggests a habitual action in the past.So, the sentence expresses a recurring action that took place in the past.

options.explanation: Monotransitive verbs are verbs that require only one object e.g. She wore a "dress".
Ditransitive verbs are verbs that require two objects e.g., He asked "her" to return "his keys".
Complex transitive verb is a verb that requires both a direct object and an object compliment. E.g., "They" named "their" new baby Giada.
A reflective transitive sentence include a pronoun that refers to and is used as a direct object of the verb

questions.explanation: Complex transitive verb is a verb that requires both a direct object and an object compliment. E.g., "They" named "their" new baby Giada.

questions.text: The sentence which has one dependent and one independent clause is called a:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A compound sentence, on the other hand, contains two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction.Example: "I went to the store, and I bought milk." (Two independent clauses: "I went to the store" and "I bought milk")

The correct answer is: complex sentence.A complex sentence is a sentence that contains one independent clause (main clause) and one or more dependent clauses (subordinate clauses). The independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, while the dependent clause cannot.Example: "I went to the store because I needed milk." (Independent clause: "I went to the store"; Dependent clause: "because I needed milk")

A compound-complex sentence contains two or more independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses.Example: "I went to the store because I needed milk, and I also bought eggs." (Two independent clauses: "I went to the store" and "I also bought eggs"; Dependent clause: "because I needed milk")
An exclamatory sentence is a sentence that expresses strong emotions or feelings, and is typically marked by an exclamation point.Example: "Wow, what a beautiful day!"

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: complex sentence.A complex sentence is a sentence that contains one independent clause (main clause) and one or more dependent clauses (subordinate clauses). The independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, while the dependent clause cannot.Example: "I went to the store because I needed milk." (Independent clause: "I went to the store"; Dependent clause: "because I needed milk")

questions.text: What is the tense of this question?Will Tom be able to mend that broken window?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The question uses “will”, which always marks future time, not present. Even though “be able” looks like a present form, the helping verb “will” decides the tense. Therefore, it cannot be present tense.
There is no past-tense verb in the sentence. Past tense would use words like was, were, could, did. This sentence uses “will”, which clearly shows future tense, so it cannot be past tense.

The question “Will Tom be able to mend that broken window?” is in the simple future tense because it uses “will” to ask about an ability or action that will happen later. The structure “will + base verb” (will be able) shows it refers to a future possibility
This option cannot be correct as option C is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following sentence is in the past perfect continuous tense:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Her friends had thought of calling the doctor when she walked in
options.explanation: The following sentence is not in the past perfect continuous tense:"Her friends had been thinking of calling the doctor when she walked in."This sentence is in the past perfect tense, but not in the past perfect continuous tense. The past perfect continuous tense is used to describe a continuous action or situation that occurred before a specific time or event in the past. It is formed using "had been" + present participle (-ing form of the verb).

options.text: It had rained heavily for several hours and the streets were very wet
options.explanation: The sentence "It had rained heavily for several hours and the streets were very wet" is in the past perfect tense, not in the past perfect continuous tense. The past perfect tense is used to indicate an action or event that happened before another past action or event. In this case, the past perfect tense is used to express that the rain had already occurred before the state of the wet streets.If we wanted to express the same idea using the past perfect continuous tense, we could modify the sentence as follows:"It had been raining heavily for several hours and the streets were very wet."In this revised sentence, "had been raining" is in the past perfect continuous tense, indicating a continuous action (rainfall) that occurred before the state of the wet streets.

options.text: Had Sam and John been cheating on the exams before the school put monitors in the classroom?
options.explanation: The sentence "Had Sam and John been cheating on the exams before the school put monitors in the classroom?" is in the past perfect continuous tense. It is used to describe an ongoing action or situation in the past that occurred before another specific past event.In this case, the sentence suggests that Sam and John were continuously cheating on exams before the school implemented monitors in the classroom. The use of "had been cheating" indicates the ongoing nature of their cheating activities prior to the implementation of monitors.The sentence implies that the action of cheating had been happening for some time before the school took action.

options.text: Did you wait long before the bus arrived?
options.explanation: The sentence "Did you wait long before the bus arrived?" is in the simple past tense. It is a question asking about a past event and does not involve the past perfect continuous tense. The verb "wait" is in the simple past tense, indicating the action of waiting in the past.

questions.explanation: The sentence "Had Sam and John been cheating on the exams before the school put monitors in the classroom?" is in the past perfect continuous tense. It is used to describe an ongoing action or situation in the past that occurred before another specific past event.In this case, the sentence suggests that Sam and John were continuously cheating on exams before the school implemented monitors in the classroom. The use of "had been cheating" indicates the ongoing nature of their cheating activities prior to the implementation of monitors.The sentence implies that the action of cheating had been happening for some time before the school took action.

A complex sentence has one independent clause and one or more dependent clauses.
Compound-complex sentence has two or more independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses.
Compound sentence has two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction.

questions.text: Demonstrate the control of tenses and sentence structure in the following sentence:This summer our vacation should be both exciting and restful.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A complex sentence is a sentence with one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. It works best when you need to provide more information to explain or modify your sentence's main point.

A simple sentence has the most basic elements that make it a sentence: a subject, a verb, and a completed thought. Examples of simple sentences include the following: Joe waited for the train. "Joe" = subject, "waited" = verb

A compound sentence is made up of two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, or so) and a comma or by a semicolon alone. Example: The pirate captain lost her treasure map, but she still found the buried treasure.

A compound-complex sentence is comprised of at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses. Example: Though Mitchell prefers watching romantic films, he rented the latest spy thriller, and he enjoyed it very much.

The parcel will be delivered is in the future indefinite tense, which is used to describe an action that will happen in the future. In this sentence, the action of delivering the parcel is going to happen in the future. This is the correct answer.

Was delivered” is simple past passive, not future indefinite.The question specifically asks you to change the sentence into future indefinite tense, so only: "The parcel will ne delivered" is correct.

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structureWho told you this?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: In English grammar, a declarative sentence is a sentence that makes a statement, provides a fact, offers an explanation, or conveys information. These types of sentences are also known as declarative statements

An imperative sentence is a sentence that expresses a direct command, request, invitations, warning, or instruction. Imperative sentences do not have a subject; instead, a directive is given to an implied second person. For example, the sentence “Wash the dinner plates” commands the implied subject to wash the dishes.

Interrogative sentences are sentences that ask a question, typically to request information. They're identified by the question mark (?) that comes at the end of the sentence instead of a period. The tricky thing about interrogative sentences is that they don't follow the same rules as other sentences

Exclamatory sentences typically start with the exclamatory pronouns “what” or “how” to emphasize an antecedent noun. For example, “What a beautiful baby!” Exclamatory sentences usually end with exclamation marks. Exclamatory sentences use this specific punctuation mark to emphasize an emotion.

questions.explanation: Interrogative sentences are sentences that ask a question, typically to request information. The tricky thing about interrogative sentences is that they don't follow the same rules as other sentences

questions.text: Identify the tense used in the given sentence:"You are always working on your laptop."
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The present indefinite tense (simple present) uses the base form of the verb (e.g., you work). In the sentence “You are always working on your laptop”, the structure “are working” shows an ongoing action, so it is present continuous tense, not present indefinite.

The present perfect tense uses “have/has + past participle” (e.g., have worked). In the sentence “You are always working on your laptop”, the verb form “are working” is present continuous, not present perfect, so present perfect tense is not correct here.
The sentence uses “are working”, which is the present continuous form (am/is/are + verb-ing).It indicates an action happening repeatedly or around the present time.

The present perfect continuous tense uses the form “have/has been + verb-ing” (e.g., You have been working). In the sentence “You are always working on your laptop”, the structure “are working” lacks “have/has been”, so it is present continuous tense, not present perfect continuous.

questions.explanation: Present continuous tense is formed through the subject + present form of the verb (‘working’) + present participle of the verb (‘are’).Here, the given sentence that is 'You are always working on your Laptop' is the present tense.As we know by the rule of grammar, the definition of the present tense is the activity that is done in the current state not in the past or the future.The person seems to be always working on his/her laptop. This is a declarative sentence.The verb 'are working' is in the present continuous tense.Hence, the correct answer is present continuous tense.

questions.text: Which tense would you use for describing next year's holiday?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Present tense is used to describe actions happening now or regular events. It can also be used for general truths or habitual actions. For example, "We go to the beach every summer" uses the present tense. However, since you're talking about next year's holiday, which is a future event, the present tense would not be appropriate here.You could use the present tense in certain contexts, like when referring to scheduled events (e.g., "The holiday starts on July 10th next year"). But typically, future tense is preferred when talking about future plans or predictions.

Past tense is used to describe events or actions that have already happened. Since next year's holiday is in the future, the past tense is not appropriate for describing it. For example, saying "Next year, we went to the beach" would be incorrect because you are referring to a time that has not yet occurred.

Future tense is used to describe actions or events that will happen in the future. Since next year refers to a time that has not yet arrived, it is logical to use the future tense to describe plans, intentions, or predictions.Example: "Next year, we will visit the mountains."This is the correct tense to use when describing next year's holiday because you're referring to something that will happen in the future.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: None of these options are correctThis option suggests that none of the provided tenses (future, past, or present) are appropriate for describing next year's holiday. However, in most cases, when talking about a holiday that will happen next year, we use the future tense to describe it. Therefore, this option is not correct, as future tense is the right choice.

questions.explanation: Future tense is used to describe actions or events that will happen in the future. Since next year refers to a time that has not yet arrived, it is logical to use the future tense to describe plans, intentions, or predictions.Example: "Next year, we will visit the mountains."This is the correct tense to use when describing next year's holiday because you're referring to something that will happen in the future.

questions.text: We will go to Dubai.Which tense is used here?

options.explanation: Present tense is a grammatical tense which expresses an action or event that is ongoing now or habitually performed, or a state that exists generally or currently. The general formula for simple present tense is: subject + first form of verb or subject + is/am/are + object.

Past tense is a grammatical tense which expresses a past action or event, or a past state of being; something that happened or existed before now.The general formula for simple past tense is: subject + second form of verb or subject + was/were + object.

Future tense is a grammatical tense which expresses an action that has not yet happened or a state that does not yet exist. The general formula for simple future tense is: subject + will + root form of verb + object.

Past perfect tense is a past tense which expresses an action in the past that happened before another action in the past. It is used to describe an event in the past that has been completed. The general formula for past perfect tense is: subject + had + past participle + object.

questions.explanation: Future tense is a grammatical tense which expresses an action that has not yet happened or a state that does not yet exist. The general formula for simple future tense is: subject + will + root form of verb + object. The use of the word “will” is associated with the future tense.

The correct answer is: complex sentence.A complex sentence is a sentence that contains one independent clause (main clause) and one or more dependent clauses (subordinate clauses). The independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, while the dependent clause cannot.Example: "I went to the store because I needed milk." options.explanation: This option does not disrupt the grammatical structure of the sentence. 'Can' is a modal verb that fits well with the rest of the sentence.

'Now' is an adverb indicating time and does not interfere with the sentence structure. Therefore, it is not the correct choice.
The sentence uses 'either' but incorrectly pairs it with 'nor' instead of 'or'. The correct conjunctions would be 'either/or' or 'neither/nor'. Therefore, 'nor' does not fit in the sentence.

questions.text: Which sentence uses capital letters correctly?
questions.subject: English

options.text: I will be moving To china.
options.explanation: In the sentence "I will be moving to China," the capitalization is used correctly for the following reasons:Proper noun: "China" is a proper noun referring to a specific country. Proper nouns, such as the names of countries, cities, or people, should be capitalized.The sentence beginning: The pronoun "I" is at the beginning of the sentence, which is another instance where capitalization is required.Capitalizing "to" is unnecessary: In this context, the word "to" is a preposition, which typically does not require capitalization unless it is the first word of a title or sentence.Therefore, the correct capitalization for the sentence is "I will be moving to China."

questions.text: Identify the type of sentence.I waited for the bus, but it was late.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence has only one idea (one independent clause)

The sentence "I waited for the bus, but it was late" is a compound sentence because it:Has two independent clauses (I waited for the bus / it was late). The two clauses are joined by a coordinating conjunction (but). Both clauses are complete sentences that could stand alone, and they are joined together using a coordinating conjunction to show a contrast between the two actions.
A complex sentence has one main (independent) clause and one or more dependent clause
A compound complex sentence has at least two independent clauses at least one dependent clause

questions.explanation: The sentence "I waited for the bus, but it was late" is a compound sentence because it:Has two independent clauses (I waited for the bus / it was late). The two clauses are joined by a coordinating conjunction (but). Both clauses are complete sentences that could stand alone, and they are joined together using a coordinating conjunction to show a contrast between the two actions.

questions.text: How cold the night is!Which type of sentence it is?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An interrogative sentence is a sentence that asks a question and ends with a question mark. As the given sentence does not contain a question, this option is incorrect.
A declarative sentence is a simple sentence, that contains a piece of information. It may be a negative sentence, but it always ends with a full stop. Hence, this option is incorrect.
An exclamatory sentence shows sudden feelings and emotions. It also ends with an exclamatory mark. It perfectly complements the given sentence, so this option is correct.
An imperative sentence is used to command, or order. The given sentence is not an imperative sentence. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: An exclamatory sentence shows sudden feelings and emotions. It also ends with an exclamatory mark. It perfectly complements the given sentence, so option C is correct.

questions.text: Identify the type of sentence given below:The caliph noticed the merchant.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause, typically introduced by words like 'because,' 'although,' and 'since.' The sentence provided lacks a dependent clause, making it simple rather than complex.

This option is incorrect. A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses joined by coordinating conjunctions such as 'and,' 'but,' or 'so.' The sentence given has only one independent clause and no conjunctions.

This option is incorrect. A compound-complex sentence includes at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses. The sentence in question has only one independent clause and no dependent clauses.

This option is correct. A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause with a subject and predicate, expressing a complete thought. 'The caliph noticed the merchant' fits this structure perfectly, as it contains one subject ('The caliph') and one predicate ('noticed the merchant').

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D: Simple. The sentence 'The caliph noticed the merchant' is a simple sentence because it has only one independent clause that expresses a complete thought. It contains a subject ('The caliph') and a verb ('noticed') that together form a straightforward statement. The other options are incorrect as they describe sentence structures involving multiple clauses, which are not present in the given sentence.

questions.text: Identify the tense of the given sentence:The sick child has been sleeping for three hours
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence uses "has been sleeping," which is the Present Perfect Continuous tense, indicating an action that started in the past and is still continuing.
The Present Perfect Tense would use "has slept," which is not in the given sentence.
The Past Perfect Tense would use "had been sleeping", as it is the helping verb used in this tense.
The Present Continuous Tense would use "is sleeping," but the sentence refers to a continued action over time.

questions.explanation: The sentence uses "has been sleeping", which is the Present Perfect Continuous tense, indicating an action that started in the past and is still continuing.

options.explanation: The sentence makes a statement and conveys information, making it declarative.
An imperative sentence gives a command, which this sentence does not.
An optative sentence expresses a wish, which is not the case here.
Exclamatory sentences express strong emotions, usually ending with an exclamation mark.

questions.explanation: The sentence makes a statement and conveys information, making it declarative.

questions.text: That is the reason why I couldn't meet you. What type of sentence is the given sentence?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence has only one independent clause, but this sentence contains both an independent and a dependent clause.
A compound sentence consists of two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction, but this sentence has a dependent clause ("why I couldn't meet you"), making it complex.

A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. In this case, "why I couldn't meet you" is a dependent clause modifying "the reason," making the sentence complex.
A compound-complex sentence has at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses, but this sentence has only one independent clause.

questions.explanation: A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. In this case, "why I couldn't meet you" is a dependent clause modifying "the reason," making the sentence complex.
